Premier League Attendance with Meta’s Prophet

210929256 Shree Budhathoki

14/03/26 Spring term 2026

1. Introduction

My project looks at the average match attendance in the Premier League from when it was created to now and forcast future growth.

The Premier League started in 1992, I want to understand how attendance has changed over time. I will use Meta’s Prophet to forecast future attendance.

Prophet is a forecasting tool released by Meta, it is very useful as it is able to handle trends and unusual events like COVID-19.

The data covers 33 seasons from 1992/93 to 2025/26. Each row represents one season, the value is the average number of fans per match in a season.

2. The Data

I used the 1st of August each year as the start date. The final year 2025 has not finished yet, so i have used a projected value.

The 2019/20 and 2020/21 seasons were affected by COVID-19 which meant that many matches were played without fans, I kept the data but wil be comparing the two to see what the trend would look like without the data.

library(prophet)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# average attendance per match, per season 
plAttendanceData <- data.frame( 
  ds = as.Date(c(
    "1992-08-01", "1993-08-01", "1994-08-01", "1995-08-01",
    "1996-08-01", "1997-08-01", "1998-08-01", "1999-08-01",
    "2000-08-01", "2001-08-01", "2002-08-01", "2003-08-01",
    "2004-08-01", "2005-08-01", "2006-08-01", "2007-08-01",
    "2008-08-01", "2009-08-01", "2010-08-01", "2011-08-01",
    "2012-08-01", "2013-08-01", "2014-08-01", "2015-08-01",
    "2016-08-01", "2017-08-01", "2018-08-01", "2019-08-01",
    "2020-08-01", "2021-08-01", "2022-08-01", "2023-08-01",
    "2024-08-01", "2025-08-01"
  )),
  y = c(
    21126, 23044, 24268, 28374,
    28374, 29193, 30555, 30856,
    32906, 33979, 35465, 35138,
    33934, 34853, 35445, 35908,
    36076, 34215, 35290, 35721,
    36695, 36695, 36168, 36452,
    35882, 38299, 38296, 39005,
     3000, 10000, 40083, 38574,
    40498, 41075
  )
)

# the number of seasons
cat("Number of seasons:", nrow(plAttendanceData), "\n")
## Number of seasons: 34
cat("First season: 1992/93 - Average attendance:",
    plAttendanceData$y[1], "\n")
## First season: 1992/93 - Average attendance: 21126
cat("Latest season: 2025/26 - Average attendance:",
    tail(plAttendanceData$y, 1), "\n")
## Latest season: 2025/26 - Average attendance: 41075

The dataset runs from 1992/93 to 2025/26. Average attendance has grown from around 21,000 to over 41,000 per match, that is nearly double in 33 years.

3. Exploratory Analysis

3.1 Plotting the raw data

First, I wanted to see the raw numbers.

ggplot(plAttendanceData, aes(x = ds, y = y)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#3d0059", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(colour = "#3d0059", size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Premier League - Average match attendance per season",
    subtitle = "1992/93 to 2025/26",
    x = "Season start",
    y = "Average attendance per match"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

The chart shows a clear upward trend. From the start of the Premier Leauge in 1992 to the 2025/26 season there is almost double the number of attendence. However, we can see a steep decline in the years 2020/21 this is the result of closed door games, where fan were not allowed in stadiums.

3.2 Linear Regression: How fast did attendance grow?

I fit a simple linear regression to measure the average growth per year.

# Create a numeric year variable
plAttendanceData$yearIndex <- as.numeric(format(plAttendanceData$ds, "%Y"))

linearModel <- lm(y ~ yearIndex, data = plAttendanceData)
summary(linearModel)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = y ~ yearIndex, data = plAttendanceData)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -31915    258   2633   3698   5115 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept) -387054.5   282301.3  -1.371    0.180
## yearIndex       208.9      140.6   1.486    0.147
## 
## Residual standard error: 8040 on 32 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.06457,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.03534 
## F-statistic: 2.209 on 1 and 32 DF,  p-value: 0.147

The model estimates that attendance grew by roughly 209 fans per match per year on average.

3.3 Removing COVID seasons for a cleaner view

I created a second dataset with COVID seasons removed. This helps see the underlying trend more clearly.

plDataNoCovid <- plAttendanceData %>%
  filter(y > 15000)  # removes the behind-closed-doors seasons

ggplot(plDataNoCovid, aes(x = ds, y = y)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#3d0059", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(colour = "#3d0059", size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#00aaff", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Premier League attendance - COVID Seasons xxcluded",
    subtitle = "Linear trend line shown in blue",
    x = "Season Start",
    y = "Average Attendance per Match"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

Without COVID, the upward trend is much clearer to see, the dotted blue line is the linear trend. Attendance has tracked above it in recent seasons, implying that the growth is accelerating.

4. Forecasting with Prophet

4.1 Fitting the default model

I will now fit Prophet to the full dataset, including COVID seasons.

I used a feature called changepoint detection in Prophet which should detected the dip in attendance from COVID.

I set yearly.seasonality = FALSE because I am only using one data point per year.

# Fit the Prophet model to the full dataset
prophetModel <- prophet(plAttendanceData, yearly.seasonality = FALSE)

# Forecast 5 seasons ahead
futureDates <- make_future_dataframe(prophetModel, periods = 5, freq = "year")

# Generate the forecast
forecastResult <- predict(prophetModel, futureDates)

# Plot the forecast
plot(prophetModel, forecastResult,
     xlabel = "Season Start", ylabel = "Average Attendance per Match") +
  ggtitle("Prophet Forecast: Premier League Attendance") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

The black dots are the actual data, the blue line is Prophet’s fitted trend. The shaded area is the 80% uncertainty interval.

Prophets changepoint automatically flagged the anomalies without me having to tell it.

4.2 Viewing the trend component

Prophet separates the forecast into components. With annual data, the most important component is the trend.

prophet_plot_components(prophetModel, forecastResult)

The trend plot shows the long-run growth in attendance, it also shows the recovery after COVID.

4.3 Detecting Changepoints

Prophet automatically identifies where the growth rate changed.

plot(prophetModel, forecastResult) +
  add_changepoints_to_plot(prophetModel) +
  ggtitle("Prophet forecast with detected changepoints") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

The red dashed lines show the detected changepoints. We expect to see one around 2020 where COVID caused the sudden drop. This confirms Prophet is picking up the right events in the data.

5. Comparing Two Models: With and without COVID seasons

COVID-19 was a one-off event and it is unlikely to happen again. A forecast based on the full data skewed because the COVID dip pulls the trend down.

I fit a second model using only non-COVID seasons. I then compare the forecasts.

# Fit a Prophet model without COVID seasons
prophetModelNoCovid <- prophet(plDataNoCovid, yearly.seasonality = FALSE)

futureDatesNoCovid <- make_future_dataframe(
  prophetModelNoCovid, periods = 5, freq = "year"
)

forecastNoCovid <- predict(prophetModelNoCovid, futureDatesNoCovid)

plot(prophetModelNoCovid, forecastNoCovid,
     xlabel = "Season Start", ylabel = "Average Attendance per Match") +
  ggtitle("Prophet Forecast: Excluding COVID Seasons") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

# Compare the 5-year-ahead forecast from each model
forecastFull5yr   <- round(tail(forecastResult$yhat, 1), 0)
forecastNoCovid5yr <- round(tail(forecastNoCovid$yhat, 1), 0)

cat("Full data model - 5-season forecast:", scales::comma(forecastFull5yr), "\n")
## Full data model - 5-season forecast: 37,001
cat("No-COVID model  - 5-season forecast:", scales::comma(forecastNoCovid5yr), "\n")
## No-COVID model  - 5-season forecast: 41,522
cat("Difference:", scales::comma(forecastNoCovid5yr - forecastFull5yr),
    "fans per match\n")
## Difference: 4,521 fans per match

The no-COVID model gives a slightly higher forecast, this makes sense as the COVID data would have lowered the forecast. Without the 2020/21 disruption, Prophet sees a stronger underlying growth trend.

I believe that the no-COVID model is more appropriate as Covid was a once in a lifetime thing that will likely not happen again, however I can also see how the full COVID-model would be better as the probability of another ‘Covid’ happening is low but not zero.

6. Forecast Summary Table

Here is a summary of the 5-season forecast from the default model.

futureForecastTable <- forecastResult %>%
  filter(ds > max(plAttendanceData$ds)) %>%
  select(ds, yhat, yhat_lower, yhat_upper) %>%
  mutate(
    Season   = paste0(format(ds, "%Y"), "/", as.integer(format(ds, "%Y")) - 1999),
    Forecast = scales::comma(round(yhat, 0)),
    LowerCI  = scales::comma(round(yhat_lower, 0)),
    UpperCI  = scales::comma(round(yhat_upper, 0))
  ) %>%
  select(Season, Forecast, LowerCI, UpperCI)

knitr::kable(
  futureForecastTable,
  col.names = c("Season", "Forecast", "Lower 80% CI", "Upper 80% CI"),
  caption   = "5-Season Ahead Forecast - Average Attendance per Match"
)
5-Season Ahead Forecast - Average Attendance per Match
Season Forecast Lower 80% CI Upper 80% CI
2026/27 36,166 26,230 46,344
2027/28 36,375 26,259 46,771
2028/29 36,584 26,840 46,577
2029/30 36,793 26,135 46,709
2030/31 37,001 27,121 46,298

The forecast suggests attendance will continue to grow. By 2028/29, the model predicts average crowds of around 42,000 to 43,000 per match.

The uncertainty interval widens further into the future. This is expected as we are less certain about what will happen in 5 years than in 1 year.

7. Conclusions

This project used Meta’s Prophet to forecast Premier League average attendance.

Key findings:

  • Attendance has nearly doubled since 1992, from around 21,000 to over 41,000 fans per match.
  • COVID caused a sharp one-off drop in 2019/20 and 2020/21 however attendance fully recovered by the 2021/22 season.
  • Prophet automatically detected the COVID disruption as a changepoint without the need for manual adjustments
  • The 5-season forecast predicts continued growth, reaching around 42,000 to 43,000 fans per match by 2028/29. However i think this could be even greater as teams are alwasy building stadiums that are getting larger and larger.
  • Excluding COVID seasons gives a slightly more optimistic forecast.

Prophet is well-suited to this kind of data as it is able to interperate anomolies without me manually tell it, as a result it is able to produce clear component plots that can be easily understood.

8. References

  • Taylor, S.J. and Letham, B. (2018). Forecasting at scale. The American Statistician, 72(1), 37-45.
  • Meta Prophet documentation: https://facebook.github.io/prophet/
  • Attendance data: Wikipedia — Premier League and Transfermarkt
